title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee approved several construction contracts and agreements, including recycled asphalt crushing, military road construction with Rothschild, and construction services for upcoming road projects. The committee also directed staff to develop enforcement language for a $100/day fine for residents illegally discharging sump pump water into sanitary sewers, addressing a significant issue that resulted in treating approximately 15 million extra gallons of clear water in April 2026.

agenda.item: Approval of April 13, 2026 Public Works/Utility Committee meeting minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permit
Review 2025 Weston Water Utility Public Service Commission annual reports
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue duplex
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Avenue projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the approximately $1.5 million project originally scheduled for 2029 but likely moving to 2028. He noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past four to five years. Discussion included upcoming repairs on Weston Avenue by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road settlement issues, and plans for manhole repairs on Schofield Avenue with HOS Construction testing different repair methods.

Staff reported on spring maintenance activities including road limit removal on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, spring curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Significant flooding required dewatering pumps near Weston Avenue for approximately one week. Discussion addressed the need for a permanent pump solution for recurring drainage issues in the Crane Meadows area. Two culverts failed on Jones Street requiring emergency repairs. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment and preparing summer operations.

Josh reported on service repairs at Caribou Coffee, ongoing private well sampling, and lift station maintenance issues including power failures. A major concern was identified: in April 2026, the village pumped 37 million gallons into the water system but treated nearly 54 million gallons of wastewater - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water from infiltration and illegal sump pump discharges. Staff caught one resident on Kathleen Lane whose sump pump was causing the small lift station to run almost continuously. The INI study with In-Pipe Technologies will help identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing operating revenue of approximately $3 million, net operating income of $482,911 (consistent with prior year's $490,000), and 6% water loss. The utility's total value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves 6,100 customers total: 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 others. The committee moved to acknowledge the report, which passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term state transportation funding solutions. He noted while the village receives good grant funding for one-time projects, recurring annual costs for street maintenance and snow plowing require general tax dollar subsidies. The motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton (versus $4.25 from competitor) for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. He demonstrated this saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to market prices, resulting in about $130,000 savings across this year's three projects. The motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcing fines for illegal sump pump discharges into sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee members distinguished between unknowing violations (first-time offenders who should be notified and given chance to correct) versus willful repeat offenders. Trustee Zygami suggested letting the In-Pipe Technologies study identify problem areas first, then sending notices with a 30-60 day correction period before fines. The committee directed staff to draft enforcement language with fines starting at $100 per day. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael explained a builder wants to construct a duplex on Lot 1 of Radkkey Avenue, but water/sewer mains don't extend to the lot. Staff proposed allowing temporary long lateral connections through Outlot 1, with the owner paying assessment when Radkkey Avenue is reconstructed and mains extended. Committee requested adding a deadline, agreeing on 'no later than end of 2029 construction season.' Motion to recommend board approval with this date added passed unanimously.

Michael presented the intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road reconstruction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Rothschild holds the contract with Steam Construction and handles street/storm water; Weston is responsible for water and sanitary sewer work totaling an estimated $94,860. The water main requires relocation due to storm sewer conflicts. The motion to recommend board approval passed unanimously.

Due to the vacant engineering technician position, staff sought construction inspection proposals. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project: MSA bid $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue: MSA bid $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730. Michael recommended MSA for the roundabout (lower cost, helped with design) but Clark Dietz for Bladel due to having a full-time experienced inspector versus MSA's summer intern for similar cost. Motion to approve MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Staff to draft enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fine
Recommend board approval of Radkkey Avenue water/sewer connection agreement with reconstruction deadline of no later than 2029
Recommend board approval of Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for estimated $94,860
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction services ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue ($73,730)
Recommend board adoption of Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Special assessment meeting scheduled for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM for Bladel Avenue project
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs for future meeting
Consider adding street lighting to Schofield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road in future CIP
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Schofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday this week

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work product transmittals and educational presentations
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair, seconded by Jackson, and elected unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary unanimously. There was brief procedural confusion about whether the Vice Chair nomination had been properly seconded.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. He reported estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year and announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making them the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commissioner Hodal asked about room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission requested better tracking of hotel room usage for future decisions. A motion to approve $5,000 (same as last year) was made and passed unanimously.

Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, noting the candidates were impressed with Marathon County. She mentioned meeting Anastasia, who won the title. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with staff conditions.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Unfinished business - Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff remarks

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Jennifer explained the building meets all B2 district zoning requirements and setbacks. Chief Jeremy discussed challenges with a gas pipeline on site, noting the building was rotated to face east and pushed north to avoid conflicts and reduce fill costs. The building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond current 40-officer staffing. An alternate storage shed was discussed as a potential add-on. Commissioner questions addressed landscaping compliance with current code, gas pipeline construction requirements, and future expansion possibilities to the north. Motion to approve with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Jennifer presented a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. A standardized worksheet similar to DOT standards will be used. Staff noted one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and should be contacted. Commissioners discussed the village's shallow wells (90-110 ft) and sandy soil making contamination risk higher. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
discussions.body: Motion to approve passed unanimously with no discussion.
Motion to approve passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Jennifer announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email invitations and calendar events.

Electric vehicle charging stations were noted for a future agenda, likely in the fall. Discussion arose about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with Ehlers having prepared a presentation. Commissioners requested the presentation be shared, possibly incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via video call. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, asking that taxpayers not be burdened with excessive landscaping costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and findings.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with two annual inspections per facility
Staff to report inspection findings to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email invitations and calendar events for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No formal votes were taken beyond approving the April 1st meeting minutes; the meeting focused on familiarizing new committee members with department missions, budgets, and organizational structures.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (remote attendance noted for Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute (no items presented)
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04 - missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Lance Leonard
Veteran Services Department presentation by VSO Erin Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date discussion and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, Vice Chair Rosenberg seconded. The motion carried with all voting in favor by voice vote.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee ordinance and structure
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction including oversight of Veteran Services, Social Services, and Health Department, plus affiliated multi-county entities North Central Health Care and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He detailed statutory reporting relationships with the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will focus on developing and monitoring a new strategic plan, targeting completion by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect from environmental hazards. She highlighted 1,200 licenses overseen, a new homeless system response position funded by the county and City of Wausau, the Nurse Family Partnership program now graduating its first two-year-olds, and public health vending machines providing free tick kits and other supplies. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE staff. Administrator Leonard noted about half of their $5 million budget is tax levy, with significant revenue from public charges for services and intergovernmental grants.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa, explaining the department's nearly 140 FTE staff handle child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program that addresses root causes of housing instability. The department's 2026 budget exceeds $22 million with about $7 million in tax levy. Leonard anticipates restructuring requests for increased staffing needs in areas like child support.

VSO Erin Galindo reported his department increased from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE and had 8,149 client interactions in 2025, up from approximately 5,900 previously. He emphasized the PACT Act has significantly increased claims and benefits, with $44 million in annual compensation flowing to local veterans and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. New initiatives include housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, suicide prevention efforts (noting 7 of Marathon County's 30 suicides in 2025 were veterans), and improving death certificate documentation for surviving spouse benefits. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have a VSO but offices collaborate well and can serve veterans from any county.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained ADRC of Central Wisconsin serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) as an integrated organization providing aging and disability resources plus Meals on Wheels. He noted significant financial challenges: federal funding has remained flat with no Older Americans Act reauthorization in over two years while nutrition costs increased 40-45% over four years. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135. The ADRC hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate nutrition program at the Hmong American Center.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full county board
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday June 3rd at 3 PM (originally misstated as May 29th)
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested future agenda items
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion to future meetings

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also appointed two new members to the CLIPS committee and amended the remote meeting attendance policy.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fee amendments
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor and e-bike regulations
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 per duplex unit to $563, and from $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke in public comment questioning the justification for the numbers without detailed operational cost data. Trustee Barb supported the fees as justified given population growth. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional inspection agreement between Safer and municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others. Food trucks inspected by any participating department receive a sticker recognized by all, eliminating duplicate inspections. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously with motion by Lope and second by Zagami.

The board debated amendments to the remote attendance policy. Discussion centered on removing language requiring specific health/safety/welfare justifications for remote attendance. Trustee Beck moved to amend the policy to strike sentences requiring detailed justifications, keeping only requirements to notify 12 hours in advance, be on camera, and not participate in closed sessions. The amendment passed with Trustees Hermling and Motti voting no. The original motion as amended then passed with the same two opposing votes.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, passed unanimously. The board discussed having committee applications shared in packets going forward.

The board held discussion-only on the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield location. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about parking on grass along Alderson. Renee explained the market grew from 25 stalls at the old location to about 53 at Kennedy Park, with vendors now able to keep vehicles with them. Mont Miller Park was discussed but rejected due to poor visibility and distance. The board acknowledged this is an ongoing situation with no immediate resolution, with some suggesting discontinuing the market if no suitable location is found.

Public Works Director Michael explained that contracts for outside engineering consultants (approximately $108,000 mentioned in public comment) are needed because the engineering technician position remains unfilled due to workforce shortages in central Wisconsin. Even local consulting firms are losing employees to relocations. The position has been advertised multiple times.

Michael reported on the speed limit issue raised at prior meetings. The DOT engineering study showed a 35 mph road, but the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph. A second traffic study is needed but was delayed due to construction. A resident noted the speed is currently inconsistent with the village ordinance. Michael indicated this would need to come back to the board.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment raising three concerns: criticism of spending $108,000 on outside engineering consultants instead of filling the vacant engineering technician position, objecting that Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before official appointment by the board, and expressing disappointment that no Kennedy Park funding information was included in the packet as previously requested. He called for more accountability from the village administrator.
actionItems: Ordinance 26-010 approved increasing park and recreation impact fees
Resolution 2026-015 approved amending village fee schedule for park fees
Ordinance 26-008 approved creating regional mobile food vendor inspection agreement
Ordinance 26-009 approved amending standing committees section
Ordinance 26-011 approved amending nuisances chapter
Ordinance 26-012 approved amending bicycle regulations for e-bikes
Resolution 2026-014 approved supporting sustainable transportation funding
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand article dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC pending background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 pm
Staff to bring Weston Avenue speed limit issue back to board after completing traffic study
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspection assistance

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected two at-large members to the Executive Committee (Supervisors Hapa and Arstead), and passed a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for debris from the recent tornado and flood disaster. The board heard public comment from affected Ringle residents requesting fee waivers and approved a reduced rate of approximately $36.94 per ton for certified disaster debris.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and silent moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Chair's remarks on committee appointments and leadership responsibilities
Committee appointments (Item B7) - motion and vote
Election of at-large Executive Committee members (Item 9B)
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase (Item D15)
Resolution 25-26: Social services budget amendment (Item D16)
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fees modification (Item E17)

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks emphasizing that 34% of the board is in their first or second term and explained the goals guiding appointments, including increasing diversity of perspectives and mentoring opportunities. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, passed but was not unanimous. The appointments included both county board chair and administrator appointments.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained she nominated Supervisor Leur because she had seen her advocate for the county's smallest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors voted for two candidates. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following the April tornado and flooding. The state is waiving $13.10/ton, the Town of Ringle is waiving $2.33/ton, and the county proposed reducing fees to approximately $36.94/ton (commercial rate minus state and town fees). The board suspended rules to consider the resolution since it came directly from the Solid Waste Management Board without going through a standing committee. After extensive discussion about future policy development and potential extension beyond 60 days, the resolution passed unanimously.
Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The consent agenda passed unanimously via voice vote.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was carried but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty, a Ringle resident, asked the county to waive fees entirely for six months, noting that the state, DNR, and Town of Ringle were all waiving their portions and that private businesses had donated over $40,000 in cleanup assistance. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, requesting fee waivers for underinsured homeowners and extended timelines since some insurance adjusters hadn't yet arrived. He mentioned approximately 44 homes were affected.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Staff to track and certify all disaster-related debris brought to landfill for potential state disaster aid documentation

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution for tornado-affected properties to the county board. The committee also received educational presentations from the Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and the town's approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezoning makes the existing structures conforming uses rather than preserving any natural area. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, Supervisor Drew seconded. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Consideration of fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic (POWTS) fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado, noting the governor's emergency declaration. Significant debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to individuals hit by disasters that aren't declared emergencies. Marilyn Bend asked about septic hookup costs. Eric Vogel asked if homeowners insurance would cover fees. Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution to the county board. Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then offered an amendment to limit the waiver to only POWTS reconnect fees ($160), excluding all other zoning and septic fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment carried. The amended motion to forward to the county board then passed, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher provided an overview of the department's operations, including the landfill's 45-year history, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy that began injecting gas in September 2025, and upcoming capital projects including an $6 million cell construction and leachate treatment facility required by state law by 2032. He noted lithium-ion battery fires occur about once a week at the facility.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie who had an emergency. He outlined the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal staff, management of 17 county parks and 30,700 acres of county forest. He noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced that Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Rezoning for James Offer and David Tract property (Town of Green Valley) approved and forwarded to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Rezoning for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidz property (Town of McMillan) approved and forwarded to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Modified fee waiver resolution (limited to POWTS reconnect fees only) forwarded to Marathon County Board of Supervisors for consideration
Staff to track costs and requests related to any fee waivers granted
Conservation Planning and Zoning to present at next meeting with Conservation Director Kirsty Heiden

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center to next month, approved kayak launch signage for installation at village parks, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising efforts and the Yellow Banks disc golf course tree removal project. Public commenters expressed opposition to dogs at the pool and concerns about Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from April 27, 2026 meeting
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously with a motion and second. No discussion was held on the minutes.

Director Sean Oster Bring presented a timeline document showing the Kennedy Park project history from the comprehensive plan approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital funds and the fundraising timeline. Oster Bring explained that Friends of Kennedy Park are fundraising for lights on the upper field, currently at approximately $84-85,000 toward a $117,000 goal, and plan to announce their total at an upcoming June tournament. The acting chair emphasized that the Friends group deserves grace as volunteers and noted master plans are meant to guide future development, not dictate exact outcomes.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and maps showing tree removal locations along the river and mat installation over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment operations. Dan explained that wood mats were used to cross the pipeline areas, with some extra mats available for possible future trail use. The mats now belong to the village.

discussions.item: Dog day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the June meeting because Chair Katrina, who had requested the item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. One public commenter, Andy (6504 [street inaudible]), had spoken against allowing dogs at the pool during public comment, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' His objection was noted for the record.

The acting chair raised concerns about graffiti on benches and garbage cans along the trail. Staff explained challenges with removal, noting the pressure washer cannot access the trail and memorial benches weigh approximately 2,000 pounds each. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, seal coating, and organizing volunteer work days. Committee member suggested community volunteers might help, noting people love the trail. No formal action was taken; staff will explore cleaning options.

Dan Korvino from Marathon County Parks (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of proposed kayak launch signs for installation at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J landings in Weston. The signs include maps, history, QR codes for river gauge information, and donation links to Marathon County Parks Foundation. Motion to approve installation of the signs passed unanimously. Signs will go to the village board for final approval.

Staff requested discussion on food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds after receiving an email request from a community member. The program would provide free signs encouraging keeping food away from play structures. Committee agreed to add this for future discussion along with the deferred dog day item.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating it's 'ridiculous' and dogs belong in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), speaking online under the name 'Vern M', criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the $8,700 expense figure 'too coincidental' and demanding to know where the $10,000 grant money and glow event fundraising proceeds are, suggesting the numbers are 'convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Dog day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June 22, 2026 meeting
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs approved for installation, forwarded to village board
Staff to explore graffiti removal options and potential anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail amenities
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be added to future agenda
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 p.m. in training room

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged budget and financial reports for April. The meeting featured an educational presentation by Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, election administration, licensing, and budget. Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down as this was his last meeting.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Staff remarks and farmers market discussion

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
discussions.body: Jessica provided an update on TIFF closure timeline, anticipating October-November completion. She explained the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, which are due October 1st. She confirmed the numbers still look good and the village will be able to pay off the debt. The process involves notifying US Bank and forwarding to DTC for bondholder payments.
The April budget status report, TIFF detail reports, and legal details report were acknowledged together. Motion to acknowledge was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an overview of the clerk's office operations, staffing (currently two full-time and one part-time employee, down from four full-time previously), election administration including Badger Book equipment replacement strategy, licensing responsibilities (51 liquor licenses), tax collection (84% collected at clerk's office totaling $22 million), pet licensing (brought back in-house from Pet Data saving approximately $5,495), and the quarterly newsletter costs ($9,000 postage, $20,000 printing). Discussion included poll worker wages with plans to increase chief inspector pay from $13 to $15/hour, the recent increase in pet licensing fees from $10 to $20 due to Marathon County requirements, and concerns about newsletter costs potentially being reduced through email distribution.

Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well, with full vendor capacity including food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and greenhouse vendors. Some challenges noted included Tuesday markets affected by cool and windy weather. Committee members raised concerns about parking congestion on Alderson and Newport streets. Staff clarified parking is restricted on the east side of Alderson but allowed on the west side and Newport.

Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as this was his last meeting, noting he and his wife are moving away from Weston but staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve.

actionItems: CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIFF bonds due October 1st
Clerk planning to request chief inspector wage increase to $15/hour for next two-year election term
Seeking additional part-time clerk's office staff member
Committee has open positions - members encouraged to recruit interested citizens
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, taking action on several ordinance amendments and property matters. Key decisions included recommending approval of nuisance code amendments, an e-bike ordinance with added emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned structure on Shore Avenue.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Department reports - Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke critically about the new farmers market location, expressing concern that parking vehicles on grass violates village code and will destroy the park. He questioned ADA compliance for handicap spaces on grass rather than asphalt, asked about the origin of fire inspections for food trucks, and criticized the age of pending code violations, stating some have been on the list for years with no visible progress.

Code Enforcement reported 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after property sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls for March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired their 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week and 39th starting in May. Two SROs work summer zoning violations for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

Committee discussed final copy of nuisance ordinance amendments including tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (switched to 14 and 10 feet). Motion by Olsen, seconded by Kimpie to recommend approval to board of trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion on proposed e-bike ordinance for Chapter 82. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bike-type vehicles) from roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee debated electric scooters, with concerns about restricting kids' transportation. Chief noted officers need clear guidelines and currently have no ordinance to enforce. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval with addition prohibiting emotos from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson initiated discussion noting Weston's 12-hour parking restriction window (4 AM to 4 PM) is far outside other municipalities' 5-hour windows. Public Works Director Michael indicated removing overnight parking entirely and signing only problem areas (6-8 spots) might work better. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. Committee decided to table the item until July to allow Michael to identify specific problem areas needing signage.

Mount Olive Church requested crosswalk between church and DC Everest high school parking lot. Staff estimated basic crosswalk cost at $2,000, lighted system at $25-30,000. Committee expressed reluctance to set precedent of paying for requested crosswalks. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka to recommend approval if Mount Olive covers installation costs passed unanimously.

Chief Hunt presented regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and proposed Weston participation. Inspected trucks receive sticker valid across all participating municipalities. Fire Marshal Marty already conducted one inspection that failed due to improper LP piping. Wausau declined to participate. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Kimpie to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue new duplex on private drive. Chief Hunt mentioned additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze order on abandoned structure. Property abandoned since 2010-2011, with chickens previously housed inside. Fire department declined training burn due to unsafe conditions. Current owners prefer village proceed with demolition; costs will be placed on tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Kimpie passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt provided extensive public comment criticizing the new farmers market location for parking vehicles on grass, questioning ADA compliance of handicap spaces on grass, asking about the origin of food truck fire inspections, and expressing concern about the age of pending code violations on the enforcement list.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 Nuisances amendments to board of trustees
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition to board of trustees
Michael to prepare list of specific areas needing parking signage for July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to board of trustees
Authorize staff to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
